_description: RustPython derive-macro attributes and the rules for deriving a Rust item's Python-facing name and reachability tier. Backs the internals-mapper's attribution and the panic-site auditor's tiering. Verified against crates/derive-impl/src/{pyclass,pymodule}.rs and the fuzzing seed tool's classify_method (rustpython-findings/tools/unwrap_scan/src/main.rs).

exposing_attributes._note: An item carrying one of these attributes is DIRECTLY Python-reachable (the `py` tier). `kind` labels the finding; `name_rule` says how the Python name is derived.

class_attributes._note: Struct/enum-level attributes that define a Python type (the payload). Read by gc-traverse for the HAS_TRAVERSE opt-in, and by the mapper for the class catalog.

options.name: name = "..." — Python type name override (else the Rust ident)
options.traverse: bare `traverse` → auto `#[derive(Traverse)]`; `traverse = "manual"` → hand-written `impl Traverse`; ABSENT → HAS_TRAVERSE=false (no GC tracking)
options.with: with(Trait, ...) on the IMPL attr composes protocol slots from trait impls (Constructor→__new__, Initializer→__init__, ...)

pyclass.traverse_absent_means: no MaybeTraverse::HAS_TRAVERSE — the type is invisible to the cycle collector; owned PyObjectRef/PyRef fields become uncollectable-cycle risk (the gc-traverse crown-jewel check)

options.skip: the ONLY accepted option — emits an empty traverse for this field

pytraverse.skip_is_a_bug_when: the skipped field's type OWNS a PyObjectRef/PyRef (see gc_managed_types.json); skipping a non-owning field (e.g. `PyRwLock<BigInt>`, an atomic, a primitive) is correct and must NOT be flagged

name_rules._note: How the mapper derives the Python-facing name from an exposing attribute.
name_rules.explicit_override: name = "..."  in the attribute args wins
name_rules.default: otherwise the Rust fn/mod ident verbatim

internal_tier._note: A free fn with NO exposing attribute, or an impl method with no exposing attribute and not in a protocol-trait impl, is `internal` — reachable only transitively. The panic-site auditor DEFAULT-SILENCES the internal tier (a panic there is not directly Python-triggerable; agent triage escalates only with a concrete reachability chain).
